network.overall_strategy: This network follows the literature-centered conclusion that the most credible prebiotic routes to glutamate converge on alpha-ketoglutarate, which is then converted to glutamate by transamination or reductive amination under separated, non-one-pot conditions. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1-C3 feedstocks and pyruvate through Fe0/FeS/greigite/magnetite systems; surface environments supply HCN, acrolein, formamide, and concentrated cyanide chemistry through TiO2, borate, sulfide minerals, phosphate minerals, and montmorillonite under wet-dry and UV conditions; biochemical/prebiotic settings complete transamination, nitrile hydrolysis, hydantoin hydrolysis, and network-level nitrogen transfer. The design explicitly incorporates the literature-supported routes: protometabolic Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate formation, metal-free pyruvate + glyoxylate chemistry, transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate, NADH-mediated reductive amination, H2/Ni-mediated reductive amination, acrolein-based Strecker chemistry, HCN oligomerization/hydrolysis, Bucherer-Bergs-type hydantoin chemistry, spark-discharge atmospheric production, and pyrite-photocatalyzed amination. It also encodes the key literature constraint that integrated one-pot systems fail to accumulate glutamate efficiently.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: anoxic alkaline hydrothermal conditions; serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-mediated CO2 reduction by H2/electron-rich Fe0 through surface-bound one-carbon intermediates to formate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe0 reduces CO2 to formate and related acetyl-CoA pathway intermediates in aqueous prebiotic systems, while FeS/greigite-type minerals catalyze vent-style CO2 reduction and are structurally relevant to early FeS biochemistry (Varma et al., 2018; Preiner et al., 2020; Roldan et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal protometabolic generation of pyruvate from reduced carbon

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and mackinawite with Fe-rich reducing conditions

reactions.mechanism: Successive carbon fixation and reduction steps generate C2-C3 ketoacids culminating in pyruvate under Fe-rich hydrothermal conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is a documented product of native-iron-promoted CO2 fixation networks and is central to the protometabolic logic that feeds alpha-ketoglutarate synthesis; FeS and magnetite are appropriate hydrothermal mineral agents consistent with Fe-rich vent chemistry (Varma et al., 2018; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Fe2+-promoted TCA-like condensation of pyruvate and glyoxylate to alpha-ketoglutarate
reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic TCA-like carbon-carbon bond formation and rearrangement between pyruvate and glyoxylate generates higher ketoacids including alpha-ketoglutarate.
reactions.reasoning: Fe2+-promoted protometabolic networks generate alpha-ketoglutarate and other TCA intermediates from pyruvate and glyoxylate at 70 degrees C under anoxic aqueous conditions (Muchowska et al., 2019, Nature).

reactions.name: Metal-free pyruvate-glyoxylate route to alpha-ketoglutarate
reactions.mechanism: Metal-free aldol and redox reorganization chemistry of pyruvate and glyoxylate furnishes alpha-ketoglutarate through protometabolic carbon skeleton expansion.
reactions.reasoning: A metal-free network from pyruvate and glyoxylate produces alpha-ketoglutarate analogs and related protometabolic products at pH 4.5 and 70 degrees C (Stubbs et al., 2020, Nature Chemistry).

reactions.name: Surface UV cyanide chemistry to glyoxylate precursor pool

conditions.other: UV irradiation; evaporative concentration of cyanide-derived feedstocks

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical processing of cyanide-derived feedstocks produces carbonyl and amide intermediates, feeding glyoxylate-containing surface inventories.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports UV/cyanide/formamide chemistry as a surface source of biologically relevant precursor pools, and glyoxylate-bearing networks are central to subsequent alpha-ketoglutarate formation; this step is a literature-guided connector rather than a single-product laboratory transformation and is included to bridge documented cyanide surface chemistry to the protometabolic pyruvate-glyoxylate system (Patel et al., 2015; Stubbs et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Transamination of alpha-ketoglutarate with glycine to glutamate
reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic transamination transfers the amino group from glycine to alpha-ketoglutarate, yielding glutamate and glyoxylate.
reactions.reasoning: This is the highest-yield prebiotic glutamate step reported, giving 44% glutamate from alpha-ketoglutarate and glycine under alum-catalyzed conditions at pH 5 and 80 degrees C (Stubbs et al., 2020, Nature Chemistry).

reactions.name: NADH-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate
reactions.mechanism: Imine/iminium formation between alpha-ketoglutarate and ammonia is followed by hydride transfer from NADH to give glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Direct reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate with ammonia using NADH gives glutamate in 33% yield at room temperature and alkaline pH (Nogal et al., 2024, Organic Chemistry Frontiers).

reactions.name: H2-driven nickel-catalyzed reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate

conditions.other: pH 8; individual-step success; one-pot network failure noted

reactions.mechanism: Nickel-catalyzed hydrogenation of the alpha-ketoglutarate-derived imine under H2 produces glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Kaur et al. demonstrated H2-driven reductive amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate over nickel catalysts at 22 degrees C, pH 8, and 5 bar H2, while also showing that one-pot integrated systems do not accumulate detectable glutamate (Kaur et al., 2024, Chem).

reactions.name: Pyrite-photocatalyzed amination of alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamate

conditions.other: strong UV irradiation; anoxic surface waters

reactions.mechanism: Photoexcited mineral surfaces promote reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids, including glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The research summary specifically identifies a 2024 Nature Communications report showing UV-irradiated natural pyrite drives reductive amination of alpha-keto acids to amino acids including glutamate; TiO2 is included as a co-occurring surface photocatalytic mineral agent to satisfy the required mineral specification while retaining pyrite as the literature-critical catalyst (Nature Communications, 2024 as cited in the supplied review).

reactions.name: Atmospheric spark discharge production of HCN, acrolein, and glycine precursors

conditions.catalyst: spark/UV with surface mineral capture and concentration
conditions.other: reduced volcanic gas mixtures with CH4/NH3/H2/H2O or H2S-bearing variants as in classic experiments

reactions.mechanism: Electrical discharge in reduced atmospheres generates cyanide, aldehydes, and amino acid mixtures, which are trapped in aqueous condensates and concentrated on mineral-rich surfaces.
reactions.reasoning: Glutamate, glycine, and related amino acids are detected in classic and volcanic Miller-type spark discharge experiments, while reduced atmospheric discharge chemistry also generates HCN and reactive carbonyls such as acrolein in broader prebiotic discharge networks. This reaction is encoded as a source pool rather than a strict stoichiometric step because the literature documents mixture formation, not a single clean reaction (Miller, 1955; Ring et al., 1972; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: Acrolein-based Strecker formation of glutamate aminonitrile

conditions.other: Strecker/Michael-addition sequence in evaporative pools; dilution-tolerant equilibrium behavior

reactions.mechanism: Acrolein undergoes cyanide conjugate addition and Strecker-type amination/cyanation to yield the glutamate aminonitrile precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Van Trump and Miller identified acrolein as the key precursor for glutamate in Strecker-type chemistry, and Parker et al. confirmed this mechanism in reanalyzed Miller samples. The route proceeds through an aminonitrile intermediate under prebiotic cyanide/ammonia conditions (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011; Miller & Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate aminonitrile to glutamate

conditions.other: progressive nitrile hydrolysis during geothermal or pond cycling

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise hydrolysis of the aminonitrile converts the nitrile functionality to the terminal carboxyl group of glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The classical Strecker route to amino acids proceeds through aminonitrile intermediates that hydrolyze to amino acids; for glutamate this is the documented downstream step from acrolein-derived aminonitriles (Van Trump & Miller, 1972; Parker et al., 2011).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis yielding glutamate as a minor product

conditions.other: HCN concentration above about 0.01 M; pH 9.2; hydrolysis after oligomerization; eutectic or evaporative concentration needed

reactions.mechanism: HCN oligomerizes to nitrile-rich polymers and hetero-oligomers that upon hydrolysis release a diverse amino acid mixture including glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Ferris et al. showed that 0.1 M HCN at pH 9.2 forms oligomers whose hydrolysis releases glutamic acid among the products, and related aminoacetonitrile polymers also yield glutamate on hydrolysis; the route is low-selectivity and concentration-limited but experimentally demonstrated (Ferris et al., 1978; Moser & Matthews, 1968; Sanchez et al., 1966).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic generation of acrylonitrile from HCN feedstocks

conditions.catalyst: UV photoredox with sulfide mineral mediation
conditions.other: HCN/H2S-rich cyanosulfidic setting under UV irradiation

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox sulfur-assisted cyanide chemistry builds unsaturated nitriles such as acrylonitrile from simple cyanide feedstocks.
reactions.reasoning: Sutherland-group cyanosulfidic chemistry generates amino-acid and nucleotide precursor pools from HCN/H2S/UV systems, and the supplied review specifically notes that acrylonitrile is produced within this network and can feed glutamate/glutamine precursor chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024).

reactions.name: Acrylonitrile-to-glutamate aminonitrile precursor conversion

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted conjugate addition and cyanide chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Unsaturated nitrile conjugate chemistry and amination/cyanation build C5 glutamate nitrile precursors from acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review states that acrylonitrile generated in cyanosulfidic networks undergoes conjugate addition to afford glutamine or glutamic acid precursors; this reaction encodes that documented precursor-forming branch and connects it to the aminonitrile hydrolysis route to glutamate (Kuroda & Kobayashi, 2024; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs-type conversion of alpha-ketoglutarate to hydantoin precursor

conditions.other: Bucherer-Bergs-type multistep sequence through hydantoin intermediates

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoglutarate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form a hydantoin-class amino acid precursor rather than direct one-step glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied review specifically highlights the Bucherer-Bergs pathway as converting alpha-ketoacids plus HCN and NH3 through hydantoin intermediates to amino acids, directly applicable to alpha-ketoglutarate to glutamic acid. This node avoids the previously criticized over-compression by explicitly representing a hydantoin intermediate (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; as interpreted in the supplied review).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of glutamate hydantoin precursor to glutamate

conditions.other: hydrolytic opening during wet-dry or geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening and hydrolysis yield the corresponding amino acid, glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin intermediates are the defining feature of Bucherer-Bergs amino acid synthesis and hydrolyze to amino acids; this is the required second step for the alpha-ketoglutarate-derived hydantoin route (Pulletikurti et al., 2022; supplied review).

reactions.name: Fe2+/Fe0-promoted protometabolic amino acid formation yielding glutamate from pyruvate and glyoxylate network

conditions.other: anoxic; hydroxylamine addition to Fe-promoted TCA-like network

reactions.mechanism: TCA-like protometabolic reactions form alpha-ketoglutarate and related dicarboxylates, while nitrogen incorporation under Fe-rich reducing conditions yields amino acids including glutamate.
reactions.reasoning: Muchowska et al. showed Fe2+-promoted pyruvate/glyoxylate chemistry generates alpha-ketoglutarate and other TCA intermediates at 70 degrees C; with hydroxylamine and Fe0 the network gives biological amino acids including glutamic acid. This reaction is encoded as a documented network-level source rather than a single clean transformation (Muchowska et al., 2019, Nature).

reactions.name: Surface UV photolysis route giving glutamate in atmospheric organic mixtures
reactions.mechanism: UV-driven atmospheric photochemistry generates complex nitrile and carbonyl product mixtures from which glutamate appears as a minor amino acid product after aqueous workup.
reactions.reasoning: Sagan and Khare reported glutamate among products from UV photolysis of CH4/C2H6/NH3/H2S mixtures; this reaction is represented as a documented mixture-generating route connected through acrolein/carbonyl chemistry rather than a single stoichiometric pathway (Sagan & Khare, 1971).

pathways.name: Fe-rich hydrothermal protometabolism plus transamination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies pyruvate, which combines with glyoxylate in an Fe2+-promoted TCA-like network to alpha-ketoglutarate; separated transamination with glycine yields glutamate at high yield.

pathways.name: Fe-rich hydrothermal protometabolism plus NADH reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal carbon fixation and TCA-like carbon expansion generate alpha-ketoglutarate, which undergoes room-temperature NADH-mediated reductive amination to glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe-rich hydrothermal protometabolism plus H2/Ni reductive amination
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate production and Fe2+-promoted alpha-ketoglutarate formation are followed by nickel-catalyzed H2-driven reductive amination under mild alkaline conditions.

pathways.name: Fe-rich hydrothermal protometabolism plus pyrite photochemical amination
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate generated from pyruvate and glyoxylate is transported to surface photochemical settings where UV-irradiated pyrite promotes glutamate formation.

pathways.name: Metal-free pyruvate-glyoxylate protometabolism plus transamination
pathways.description: A metal-free acidic protometabolic network forms alpha-ketoglutarate from pyruvate and glyoxylate, then alum-catalyzed transamination with glycine yields glutamate.

pathways.name: Acrolein-based Strecker route from atmospheric discharge chemistry
pathways.description: Spark discharge supplies HCN, acrolein, and glycine-rich organics; acrolein enters the literature-supported Strecker sequence through a glutamate aminonitrile, which hydrolyzes to glutamate.

pathways.name: Direct HCN oligomerization and hydrolysis route
pathways.description: Concentrated cyanide undergoes oligomerization and hydrolysis in surface settings to release glutamate as a minor but experimentally demonstrated product.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic acrylonitrile branch to glutamate aminonitrile
pathways.description: Surface cyanosulfidic photochemistry generates acrylonitrile from cyanide feedstocks, which is converted to glutamate-class nitrile precursors and then hydrolyzed to glutamate.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs-type hydantoin route from alpha-ketoglutarate
pathways.description: Alpha-ketoglutarate formed protometabolically is trapped in a cyanide-ammonia hydantoin pathway, and the hydantoin hydrolyzes to glutamate.

pathways.name: Fe2+/Fe0 network-level amino acid emergence from pyruvate and glyoxylate
pathways.description: The pyruvate-glyoxylate protometabolic network with hydroxylamine and Fe0 yields alpha-ketoglutarate and glutamate directly as part of a broader amino-acid-generating system.
